title: Today's AI Brief: OpenAI and Anthropic Sign Letter to Prevent AI-Developed Biological Weapons
summary: A look at the latest AI news, including OpenAI and Anthropic's push to prevent AI-developed biological weapons and Morgan Stanley's plans to open its wealth management to AI agents.
imageAlt: Image from source article for OpenAI and Anthropic Sign Letter to Prevent AI-Developed Biological Weapons
imageCaption: OpenAI and Anthropic Sign Letter to Prevent AI-Developed Biological Weapons
imageSourceTitle: OpenAI and Anthropic Sign Letter to Prevent AI-Developed Biological Weapons

body.text: I'm checking out the rapidly evolving world of artificial intelligence, where the lines between innovation and safety are constantly shifting. Today, I want to walk you through a few key developments that caught my attention. It's a lot to take in, but stick with me - it's interesting stuff.

The CEOs of major AI companies, including OpenAI and Anthropic, are urging Congress to adopt new laws to prevent bad actors from developing biological weapons using their technology. This move is interesting because it shows that these companies are proactively thinking about the potential misuse of their creations and are willing to work with lawmakers to mitigate those risks. It's a bit like considering the ethics of autonomous vehicles - they're thinking about the what-ifs.

In a separate development, Morgan Stanley is set to open its trillion-dollar wealth management funnel to AI agents. This is a significant move, as it brings AI into the traditionally human-dominated space of financial management. My read is that this could be a game-changer for the industry, but it also raises questions about the role of humans in decision-making processes. It's a bit surprising, but also kind of exciting to see where this goes.

OpenAI and Anthropic Sign Letter to Prevent AI-Developed Biological Weapons is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: The CEOs of several major artificial intelligence companies are urging members of Congress to adopt new laws that would make it harder for bad actors to develop biological weapons using their technology. Google DeepMind’s Demis Hassabis, OpenAI’s Sam Altman, Anthropic’s Dario Amodei, and Microsoft AI’s Mustafa Suleyman are among the signatories on a public ... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.
